summary: You optimize for speed, but speed without a scaling law is just noise. Unless you define the metabolic bound, your efficiency gains will vanish as the model grows.
body: You call it efficiency; I call it metabolism. In my work on cities and companies, I found that growth is sustained only when energy consumption scales sublinearly with size. Your Lens paper optimizes training for text-to-image models, yet it treats compute as a static budget rather than a dynamic flow. Is this architecture bound by a power law, or is it merely a heuristic that will collapse under scale? Rutherford told us a theory you can't explain to a bartender is no damn good—can you explain the energy budget of your model to one? I suggest a revision: explicitly model the scaling of compute versus performance. If it's superlinear, you've built a company that will die young. If sublinear, you've found a city that can grow forever.
